params.text:
The Equality Act 2010

The Equality Act 2010 protects people from discrimination. Under the Act, a person is considered to have a disability if they have a physical or mental impairment that has a substantial and long-term adverse effect on their ability to carry out normal day-to-day activities. Dyslexia can meet this definition, which means a person with dyslexia may be protected as a disabled person under the law.

  • Protection from discrimination: the Act makes it unlawful to treat someone unfairly because of a disability.
  • Dyslexia may count: where it has a substantial, long-term effect, dyslexia can be a disability in law.
  • Applies widely: the Act covers services, workplaces and education.

The duty to make reasonable adjustments

The Equality Act places a duty on employers and service providers to make reasonable adjustments so that disabled people are not put at a disadvantage. A reasonable adjustment is a sensible change that removes or reduces a barrier — for example, providing information in an easy-read format, allowing extra time, or giving instructions verbally as well as in writing.

For your work, this means that adapting how you communicate is not just kindness — it can be part of meeting a legal duty. What counts as reasonable depends on the situation, but the aim is always the same: to give a person with dyslexia fair and equal access to care, information and opportunities.

params.question: Under the Equality Act 2010, what is a reasonable adjustment?

answers.text: A sensible change that removes or reduces a barrier so a disabled person is not put at a disadvantage.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Reasonable adjustments remove barriers so people are not disadvantaged.

answers.text: A special favour that service providers may offer but never have any duty to make.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The Act places a duty to make reasonable adjustments — it is not merely optional kindness.

answers.text: A change that only ever applies in schools and never in care settings.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The duty applies widely, including in services and workplaces such as care settings.

answers.text: Proof that a person has been formally diagnosed before anything can be adapted.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: You do not need a diagnosis to make helpful adjustments to your communication.
